note_data.title: Temperature
keyPoints: **Temperature** is a measure of the average kinetic energy of molecules in a substance
**Thermal equilibrium** exists when two objects in thermal contact have no net flow of thermal energy between them
Objects in thermal equilibrium have the same **temperature**
The **Celsius scale** defines 0°C as the ice point and 100°C as the steam point of pure water at standard atmospheric pressure
The **Kelvin scale** is the absolute temperature scale where 0 K represents absolute zero
**Absolute zero** (0 K = -273°C) is the temperature at which molecules have minimum kinetic energy
A **thermometer** measures temperature by using a physical property that varies with temperature
**Sensitivity** of a thermometer is the change in the thermometric property per unit change in temperature
**Range** of a thermometer is the minimum and maximum temperatures it can measure accurately

definitions.definition: A measure of the average kinetic energy of molecules in a substance
The state when two objects in thermal contact have no net flow of thermal energy between them and are at the same temperature
The temperature (0 K or -273°C) at which molecules have minimum kinetic energy and all molecular motion ceases
A physical property that varies continuously and measurably with temperature

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give exact definition. For temperature, always mention 'average kinetic energy of molecules'

modelAnswer.question: A liquid-in-glass thermometer is used to measure the temperature of hot water in Lahore during summer. Explain why the thermometer reading initially decreases before increasing to the correct value.
modelAnswer.answer: Initially the glass bulb is in contact with the hot water and expands first, causing the liquid level to drop. The liquid then absorbs thermal energy from the hot water through the glass. As the liquid warms up and expands, the level rises to give the correct temperature reading.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Liquid then expands more than glass as it heats up

commonMistakes: Never say 'heat energy' — always say 'thermal energy' or 'internal energy'
Students confuse temperature with thermal energy - temperature is average KE, not total energy
Never say 'molecules stop moving at absolute zero' — say 'molecules have minimum kinetic energy'
Students forget that thermal equilibrium means 'no net flow' not 'no flow at all'
Don't write °K — the correct unit is K (kelvin) without the degree symbol
examinerTips: Temperature questions appear frequently in Paper 2 multiple choice
Always state the thermometric property when describing how a thermometer works
Thermal equilibrium questions often test understanding of energy flow direction
Remember: T(K) = T(°C) + 273 conversion is commonly tested
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Liquid-in-glass thermometer showing bulb, capillary tube, scale markings, and liquid column. Label the thermometric property as 'length of liquid column' or 'volume of liquid'.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to explain why a clinical thermometer has a constriction in the bore and why it can measure body temperature accurately
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q8 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which property of a substance varies linearly with temperature for a resistance thermometer
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — students had to compare liquid-in-glass thermometers with thermocouple thermometers, including sensitivity and range
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (4 marks) — question required explanation of how a bimetallic strip works and why different metals are needed

workedExample.question: A student uses a mercury-in-glass thermometer to measure the temperature of melting ice and boiling water at standard atmospheric pressure. The mercury column lengths are 2.5 cm and 27.5 cm respectively. Calculate the temperature when the mercury column length is 15.0 cm. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the fixed points - ice point = 0°C with length 2.5 cm, steam point = 100°C with length 27.5 cm
Step 2: Calculate the length difference between fixed points = 27.5 - 2.5 = 25.0 cm
Step 3: Find the length above ice point for unknown temperature = 15.0 - 2.5 = 12.5 cm
workedExample.answer: The temperature when the mercury column length is 15.0 cm is 50°C
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying fixed points, 1 mark for calculating range (25.0 cm), 1 mark for finding length above ice point (12.5 cm), 1 mark for correct final answer (50°C)

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused temperature and heat, using the terms interchangeably' — always specify that temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles, not total thermal energy
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to mention that thermometric properties must vary linearly with temperature' — always state linear variation when explaining how thermometers work

Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was stating that clinical thermometers read up to 100°C' — remember clinical thermometers have a limited range of approximately 35°C to 42°C for safety and accuracy
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw and label a clinical thermometer showing: glass bulb containing mercury at bottom, narrow capillary tube (bore) with constriction near bulb, scale marked from 35°C to 42°C, and kink/constriction clearly marked with explanation that it prevents mercury from falling back into bulb automatically, allowing reading to be taken after removal from patient.
note_data.quickRecap: Temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles and is measured using thermometers with thermometric properties that vary linearly with temperature. Common types include mercury-in-glass (wide range, accurate), clinical (body temperature, has constriction), and resistance thermometers (electrical resistance changes). Always remember temperature ≠ heat, and thermometric substances must expand/change uniformly with temperature for accurate readings.
